defi.amms.card1.text: A large trade moves the pool's price more than a small one — this is where slippage comes from.
defi.amms.card1.title: Price is reactive
defi.amms.card2.text: You always trade against the pool itself, not another specific person.
defi.amms.card2.title: No counterparty needed
defi.amms.card3.text: If a pool's price drifts from the wider market, traders are incentivized to correct it for profit.
defi.amms.card3.title: Arbitrage keeps it honest
defi.amms.closing: This same mechanism is also what creates impermanent loss for liquidity providers, explained later in this lesson.
defi.amms.p1: An automated market maker is the formula a liquidity pool uses to price trades, based only on the pool's own reserves — no order book, and no external price feed required for the trade itself.
defi.amms.p2: The most common model keeps the product of both reserves constant: as a trade removes some of one asset, the pool automatically raises its price relative to the other, in proportion to how much was removed.
defi.amms.title: Automated Market Makers (AMMs)
defi.dexs.cex.text: A company custodies your funds, matches orders on an internal order book, and requires an account.
defi.dexs.cex.title: CEX
defi.dexs.closing: Both models coexist across the industry — a DEX simply removes the custodian from the trading process.
defi.dexs.dex.text: Your wallet keeps custody of your funds. Trades execute against a smart contract, with no account required.
defi.dexs.dex.title: DEX
defi.dexs.p1: A decentralized exchange lets people trade assets directly from their own wallets, using smart contracts to execute trades instead of a company holding custody of funds and matching orders internally.
defi.dexs.p2: Traditional exchanges (CEXs) use an order book, matching buyers and sellers at agreed prices. Most DEXs use a different model entirely — liquidity pools priced algorithmically by an automated market maker, covered in the next two sections.
defi.dexs.title: Decentralized Exchanges (DEXs)
defi.glossary.def1: Decentralized finance: financial services rebuilt as open smart contracts instead of company-run services.
defi.glossary.def10: When a project's creators abandon it and drain its liquidity, leaving other participants with worthless holdings.
defi.glossary.def2: Self-executing code deployed on a blockchain that defines exactly how a protocol behaves.
defi.glossary.def3: Decentralized Exchange — a trading protocol that executes trades against smart contracts instead of a company's order book.
defi.glossary.def4: A smart contract holding two or more assets that traders swap against directly.
defi.glossary.def5: Automated Market Maker — the formula a liquidity pool uses to price trades based on its own reserves.
defi.glossary.def6: A token representing a liquidity provider's share of a pool, used to withdraw their deposit and earned fees.
defi.glossary.def7: Actively moving assets between protocols to capture the highest available return.
defi.glossary.def8: Locking an asset in a network or protocol's contract in exchange for rewards.
defi.glossary.def9: The gap between holding assets and providing them to a pool, caused by price divergence between reserves.
defi.glossary.title: Glossary
defi.hero.description: Open, permissionless financial protocols — how trading, lending and earning yield work without a bank or exchange in the middle.
defi.hero.stat1.label: Difficulty
defi.hero.stat2.label: Duration
defi.hero.stat3.label: Format
defi.hero.stat4.label: Sections
defi.hero.title: DeFi Fundamentals
defi.impermanentLoss.closing: The loss is called "impermanent" because it can shrink back to zero if prices return to their original ratio before you withdraw — but it becomes permanent the moment you do.
defi.impermanentLoss.field1.label: Deposited
defi.impermanentLoss.field2.label: If Held
defi.impermanentLoss.field3.label: If Withdrawn
defi.impermanentLoss.field4.label: Impermanent?
defi.impermanentLoss.field4.value: Only realized if you withdraw during the gap
defi.impermanentLoss.p1: Impermanent loss is the gap between what a liquidity provider's deposited assets would be worth if simply held, versus what they're worth withdrawn from the pool, after the pool's prices have moved.
defi.impermanentLoss.p2: It happens because an AMM automatically rebalances a pool's reserves as prices shift — the pool ends up holding more of the asset that fell in relative price and less of the one that rose.
defi.impermanentLoss.title: Impermanent Loss
defi.introduction.p1: Every financial action covered so far assumed you were the only one involved — holding an asset, moving it across a network. Decentralized finance (DeFi) introduces other participants: traders, liquidity providers, borrowers and lenders, all interacting through code instead of an institution.
defi.introduction.p2: This lesson explains what DeFi actually replaces, how a decentralized exchange fills a trade without an order book, how liquidity pools and automated market makers set prices, and the real risks — like impermanent loss — that come with participating.
defi.introduction.p3: By the end of this lesson you will understand how a swap executes without a company matching orders, why liquidity providers earn fees, what yield farming and staking actually mean in DeFi, and how to weigh the risk of a protocol before using it.
defi.introduction.title: Introduction
defi.liquidityPools.closing: The relationship between the two reserves is exactly what an automated market maker uses to set price, covered next.
defi.liquidityPools.field1.label: Pool
defi.liquidityPools.field2.label: Reserve A
defi.liquidityPools.field3.label: Reserve B
defi.liquidityPools.field4.label: LP Fee
defi.liquidityPools.p1: A liquidity pool is a smart contract holding two (or more) assets, deposited by liquidity providers (LPs), that traders swap against directly. Instead of matching a buyer with a seller, a trade adjusts the pool's own reserves.
defi.liquidityPools.p2: In exchange for depositing assets, liquidity providers receive LP tokens representing their share of the pool, and earn a portion of the trading fees generated by every swap.
defi.liquidityPools.title: Liquidity Pools
defi.meta.title: DeFi Fundamentals — Nodra Academy
defi.quiz.intro: Check your understanding of this lesson before moving on. Incorrect answers point you straight back to the relevant section.
defi.quiz.q1: 1. What does a smart contract guarantee, compared to a company-run service?
defi.quiz.q1.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Smart Contracts and try again.
defi.quiz.q1o1: A. Its published logic runs exactly as written, without hidden exceptions
defi.quiz.q1o2: B. It always doubles investor returns
defi.quiz.q1o3: C. It requires no gas fees to use
defi.quiz.q1o4: D. It can be paused only by its original creator
defi.quiz.q2: 2. How does a typical DEX differ from a centralized exchange?
defi.quiz.q2.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Decentralized Exchanges and try again.
defi.quiz.q2o1: A. It uses the same order book model, just online
defi.quiz.q2o2: B. Trades execute against a smart contract instead of a company matching orders
defi.quiz.q2o3: C. It requires users to open a custodial account
defi.quiz.q2o4: D. It cannot process token swaps
defi.quiz.q3.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Liquidity Pools and try again.
defi.quiz.q3o1: A. A single trader willing to buy at any price
defi.quiz.q3o2: B. A smart contract holding assets that traders swap against directly
defi.quiz.q3o3: C. A centralized order book matching engine
defi.quiz.q3o4: D. A type of wallet used only for staking
defi.quiz.q4: 4. What determines the price of a trade in an AMM-based pool?
defi.quiz.q4.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Automated Market Makers and try again.
defi.quiz.q4o1: A. A company-set reference price updated daily
defi.quiz.q4o2: B. The pool's own reserves, following a pricing formula
defi.quiz.q4o3: C. The average price across every exchange
defi.quiz.q4o4: D. A vote among liquidity providers
defi.quiz.q5: 5. Which of these is a real source of yield farming returns?
defi.quiz.q5.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Yield Farming and try again.
defi.quiz.q5o1: A. Trading fees earned from swaps in a pool you've provided liquidity to
defi.quiz.q5o2: B. Guaranteed fixed interest set by a government
defi.quiz.q5o3: C. Free tokens with no underlying source
defi.quiz.q5o4: D. Interest paid by the blockchain's protocol layer for holding any asset
defi.quiz.q6: 6. What is "protocol staking," as distinct from network staking?
defi.quiz.q6.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Staking and try again.
defi.quiz.q6o1: A. Locking a network's native coin to help secure that network
defi.quiz.q6o2: B. Depositing an asset into a specific protocol's contract to earn that protocol's own rewards
defi.quiz.q6o3: C. Buying insurance against smart contract risk
defi.quiz.q6o4: D. Registering a wallet address with a centralized exchange
defi.quiz.q7: 7. When does impermanent loss become permanent?
defi.quiz.q7.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review Impermanent Loss and try again.
defi.quiz.q7o1: A. The moment prices in the pool start to diverge
defi.quiz.q7o2: B. The moment you withdraw your liquidity while prices are diverged
defi.quiz.q7o3: C. It is always permanent, regardless of when you withdraw
defi.quiz.q7o4: D. Only if the protocol is exploited
defi.quiz.q8.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review DeFi Risks and try again.
defi.quiz.q8o1: A. A sudden but harmless change in a pool's trading fee
defi.quiz.q8o2: B. A project's creators abandoning it and draining its liquidity
defi.quiz.q8o3: C. A required security audit before launch
defi.quiz.q8o4: D. A type of yield farming reward
defi.quiz.q9: 9. Why doesn't removing a company from a financial service eliminate risk?
defi.quiz.q9.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite — review DeFi Risks and try again.
defi.quiz.q9o1: A. Because smart contracts are always audited perfectly
defi.quiz.q9o2: B. Because it shifts the risk toward things like contract bugs, oracle manipulation, and impermanent loss
defi.quiz.q9o3: C. Because DeFi protocols are legally insured
defi.quiz.q9o4: D. Because all DeFi protocols are identical
defi.quiz.title: Quiz
defi.risks.intro: Removing a company from the process doesn't remove risk — it changes what kind of risk you're taking on.
defi.risks.li1: Smart contract risk. A bug in a contract's code can be exploited, and losses are frequently irreversible.
defi.risks.li2: Impermanent loss. Covered in the previous section — a real cost of providing liquidity, not just a hypothetical one.
defi.risks.li3: Oracle risk. Protocols that rely on external price feeds can be manipulated if that feed is inaccurate or compromised.
defi.risks.li4: Rug pulls. A project's creators abandon it and drain its liquidity, sometimes by design from the start.
defi.risks.li5: Composability risk. Protocols building on top of each other means a failure in one can cascade into every protocol depending on it.
defi.risks.title: DeFi Risks
defi.smartContracts.card1.text: Given the same inputs, a smart contract always produces the same outcome.
defi.smartContracts.card1.title: Deterministic
defi.smartContracts.card2.text: Anyone can read a contract's code and verify what it actually does.
defi.smartContracts.card2.title: Public
defi.smartContracts.card3.text: Most deployed contracts can't be changed, though some include upgrade mechanisms worth checking for.
defi.smartContracts.card3.title: Immutable (usually)
defi.smartContracts.p1: A smart contract is self-executing code deployed on a blockchain. Once deployed, its logic runs exactly as written — no company can quietly change the rules, freeze a specific user's funds, or make an exception behind the scenes.
defi.smartContracts.p2: Every DeFi protocol — every exchange, lending market, or yield strategy — is ultimately a set of smart contracts. Calling a protocol "trustless" means you're trusting published, inspectable code instead of a company's internal promises.
defi.smartContracts.title: Smart Contracts
defi.staking.closing: Read a staking contract's terms carefully — lock-up periods and reward sources vary significantly between protocols.
defi.staking.network.text: Securing a proof-of-stake network directly, earning a share of newly issued coins.
defi.staking.network.title: Network Staking
defi.staking.p1: In DeFi, "staking" is used in two related but distinct ways: locking a network's native coin to help secure that network, or depositing an asset into a protocol's own contract to earn rewards.
defi.staking.p2: Protocol-level staking is common: depositing an LP token to earn extra incentive rewards, or locking a governance token for a period in exchange for a share of protocol fees.
defi.staking.protocol.text: Depositing an asset into a specific protocol's contract to earn that protocol's own rewards.
defi.staking.protocol.title: Protocol Staking
defi.staking.title: Staking
defi.summary.p1: DeFi rebuilds trading, lending, and earning yield as open smart contracts instead of company-run services. Decentralized exchanges use liquidity pools priced by automated market makers, replacing a traditional order book.
defi.summary.p2: Liquidity providers earn fees but take on impermanent loss when pool prices diverge. Yield farming and staking describe different ways of putting assets to work across protocols — and every one of these activities carries risk that doesn't disappear just because there's no company involved.
defi.summary.title: Summary
defi.toc.title: Contents
defi.yieldFarming.closing: Advertised returns often combine several of these sources — and incentive rewards specifically tend to fade as a protocol matures.
defi.yieldFarming.intro: Yield farming means actively moving assets between DeFi protocols to capture the highest available return, which can come from more than one source at once.
defi.yieldFarming.li1: Trading fees. A share of the fees generated by swaps in a pool you've provided liquidity to.
defi.yieldFarming.li2: Lending interest. Interest paid by borrowers in a lending market, covered in Module 06.
defi.yieldFarming.li3: Incentive rewards. Extra tokens a protocol distributes to attract liquidity, on top of its normal fees.
defi.yieldFarming.title: Yield Farming
